title: Haryanka Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 542 to 410 BCE.
Bimbisara was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Rajagriha.
Gautam Buddha was a contemporary of this dynasty.
The succession of rulers followed the order: Ajatashatru → Udayin → Nagadasaka.
Nagadasaka was the last ruler of this dynasty.
This dynasty ruled over the region of Magadha.

title: Shishunaga Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 410 to 340 BCE.
Shishunaga was the founder of this dynasty.
Kalashoka succeeded Shishunaga as ruler.
Kalashoka was the last important ruler of this dynasty.
This dynasty ruled over the region of Magadha.

title: Nanda Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 340 to 320 BCE.
Mahapadma Nanda was the founder of this dynasty.
Dhanananda was the last ruler of this dynasty.
Alexander the Great was a contemporary of this dynasty.
This dynasty ruled over Magadha and North India.

title: Maurya Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 322 to 180 BCE.
Chandragupta Maurya was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Pataliputra, also known as Kumrahar.
Seleucus Nicator and Megasthenes were contemporaries of Chandragupta Maurya.
Chandragupta Maurya was a patron of Jainism.
Brihadratha was the last ruler of this dynasty.
Bindusara held the title Amitraghata as mentioned by the Greeks.
Antiochus I of Syria was a contemporary of Bindusara.
Bindusara was a patron of the Ajivikas.
Ashoka was a patron of Buddhism.
Upagupta was a spiritual influence on Ashoka.
Pushyamitra Sunga, the founder of the succeeding dynasty, served as the commander-in-chief under the last ruler Brihadratha.

title: Sunga Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 180 to 70 BCE.
Pushyamitra Sunga was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Vidisha, located in Madhya Pradesh.
Pushyamitra Sunga was previously the commander-in-chief of Brihadratha.
Agnimitra was a subsequent ruler, mentioned in Kalidasa's drama Malvikagnimitra.
Devabhuti was the last ruler of this dynasty.

title: Kanva Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 70 to 30 BCE.
Vasudeva was the founder of this dynasty.

title: Satavahana Dynasty
provisions: Simuka was the founder of this dynasty.
The succession of notable rulers included Satakarni I, Hala, Gautamiputra Satakarni, and Vasisthiputra Satakarni.
Pulumavi was the last ruler of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Pratishthana, also known as Paithan, in Maharashtra.

title: Mahameghavahana / Kalinga (Chedi) Dynasty
provisions: The primary source of information about this dynasty is the Hathigumpha inscription of Kharavela.
Kharavela is the most notable ruler associated with this dynasty.

title: Indo-Greeks
provisions: This group was prominent in the 2nd century BCE.
Menander, also known as Milinda, was the most famous ruler associated with this group.
Menander was a patron of Buddhism and is associated with the Buddhist scholar Nagasena.

title: Saka (Western Kshatrapa)
provisions: This dynasty ruled from the 1st century BCE to the 4th century CE.
Rudradaman was the most famous ruler of this dynasty.
Rudradaman was a patron of Sanskrit.

title: Parthians
provisions: This group was prominent from the 1st century BCE to the 1st century CE.
Gondophernes was the most famous ruler associated with this group.

title: Kushana Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from the 1st century CE to the 3rd century CE.
Kujula Kadphises and Kadphises I were among the early founders of this dynasty.
Kanishka, known as the Second Ashoka, was one of the most prominent rulers of this dynasty.
Kanishka was a patron of Buddhism and presided over the Fourth Buddhist Council.
Vasudeva I was the last ruler of this dynasty.
Notable contemporaries and scholars associated with this dynasty include Vasumitra, Ashvaghosha, Nagarjuna, Parsva, and Charaka.
Mathura was an important center during the reign of this dynasty.

title: Chera Kingdom (Sangam Period)
provisions: The capital of this kingdom was Vanchi, also known as Karur.
Udayan Cheral was the earliest known ruler of this kingdom.
Senguttuvan, known as the Red Chera, was the most famous ruler of this kingdom.
Muziris and Tondi were important ports of this kingdom.
This kingdom was associated with the Pattini cult, worshipping the goddess of chastity, Kannagi.

title: Chola Kingdom (Sangam Period)
provisions: This kingdom was prominent from the 1st century CE to the 3rd century CE.
The capitals of this kingdom were Uraiyur and Puhar.
Puhar served as an important port of this kingdom.
Elara was an early ruler of this kingdom.
Karikala was the greatest ruler of this kingdom during the Sangam period.

title: Pandya Kingdom (Sangam Period)
provisions: Mudukudumi was the earliest known ruler of this kingdom.
Nedunjeliyan was the greatest ruler of this kingdom.
The capital of this kingdom was Madurai.

title: Kadamba Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from approximately 345 CE to 540 CE.
Mayurasharma was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Banavasi, located in Karnataka.
Ravivarma was the last ruler of this dynasty.

title: Gupta Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 319 AD to 540 AD.
Sri Gupta was the original founder of this dynasty.
Chandragupta I was the first independent founder and held the title Maharaja Dhiraja.
The capital of this dynasty was initially Pataliputra, later shifted to Ujjain.
Kumaragupta was the last ruler of this dynasty.
Chandragupta II held the title Vikramaditya.
The Chinese scholar Fa-Heen visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II.
Kumaragupta is credited with founding Nalanda University.
Skandagupta is known for defeating the Huns.

title: Vakataka Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from the 3rd century AD to the 5th century AD.
Vindhyashakti was the founder of this dynasty.
Pravara Sena I is considered the real founder who consolidated the dynasty.
This dynasty succeeded the Satavahanas.
This dynasty was preceded by the Chalukyas.
The rulers of this dynasty were followers of Shaivism.

title: Pushyabhuti / Vardhana Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 550 to 650 AD.
Pushyabhuti was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Thaneswar.
This dynasty succeeded the Guptas.
Prabhakaravardhana was the first important ruler of this dynasty.
Harshavardhana ruled from 606 to 647 AD.
Harshavardhana succeeded Rajyavardhana, who was killed by Shashanka of Gauda in north-western Bengal.
Harshavardhana held the titles Siladitya and Sakalottarapathanatha.
Kannauj became the capital after Harshavardhana drove out Shashanka.
The Chinese traveller Hiuen Tsang visited India during the reign of Harshavardhana.
Harshavardhana was originally a Shaivite but later embraced Mahayana Buddhism.
Harshavardhana was defeated by Pulakesin II.

title: Chalukya Dynasty (Vatapi / Badami)
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 540 to 750 AD.
Pulakesin I was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Vatapi, also known as Badami.
Hiuen Tsang was a contemporary of this dynasty.
The Rashtrakutas, who were feudatories of this dynasty, eventually succeeded them.
Kirtivarman II was the last ruler of this dynasty.

title: Sasanka (Gauda) Kingdom
provisions: This kingdom existed from approximately 590 CE to 625 CE.
Sasanka was the founder of this kingdom.
The capital of this kingdom was Karnasuvarna, located in Murshidabad, West Bengal.
Manava was the last ruler of this kingdom.
Sasanka is known for killing Rajyavardhana, the brother of Harshavardhana.

title: Karakota Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from approximately 625 CE to 855 CE.
Durlabhavardhana was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Parihasapura, later Srinagar, in Kashmir.
Shankaravarman was the last ruler of this dynasty.

title: Maukhari Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty was prominent in the 6th century CE to the early 7th century CE.
Harivarman was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Kannauj, in Uttar Pradesh.
Grahavarman was the last ruler of this dynasty.

title: Pallava Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 600 to 900 CE, with earlier lines dating back to 250 CE.
Vishnugopa was a king of the second line who was defeated by Samudragupta.
The capital of this dynasty was Kanchipuram.
Aparajita was the last ruler, defeated by the Chola ruler Aditya I.
Mahendravarman I ruled from 600 to 630 CE and initiated conflict with the Chalukyas.
Mahendravarman I was initially a follower of Jainism but converted to Shaivism under the influence of a Nayanar saint.
Mahendravarman I held the title Chitrakarapuli.
Narasimhavarman I ruled from 630 to 670 CE and held the titles Mamalla and Vatapikonda.
Narasimhavarman I defeated Pulakesin II at the Battle of Manimangalam.
Hiuen Tsang was a contemporary of Narasimhavarman I.
Narasimhavarman II ruled from 695 to 720 CE and held the titles Rajasimha, Shankara Bhakta, Agamapriya, and Vadyavidyadhara.
The scholar Dandin was a contemporary of Narasimhavarman II.

title: Chahamana / Chauhan Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty was prominent from the 7th century to the 11th century.
Simharaja and Vasudeva were among the early founders of this dynasty.
The rulers of this dynasty held the title Maharajadhiraja.
The capital of this dynasty was Delhi.
This dynasty served as feudatories of the Pratiharas.
Vigraharaja IV ruled from 1152 to 1164 AD.
Prithviraja III ruled from 1170 to 1190 AD and held the title Rai Pithora.

title: Chandella Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty was prominent from the 8th century to the 12th century.
Nanuka was the founder of this dynasty.
The capitals of this dynasty included Khajuraho, Mahoba, and Kalinjar.

title: Kalachuri Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty was prominent from the 8th century to the 12th century.
Kokalla I was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Chedi, also known as Tripuri.
The poet Rajashekhara was a contemporary of this dynasty.

title: Paramara Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty was prominent from the 7th century to the 11th century.
Siyaka II and Sri Harsha were among the early founders of this dynasty.
The capitals of this dynasty were Ujjain and Dhar.
This dynasty served as feudatories of the Rashtrakutas.
King Bhoja was the most famous ruler of this dynasty.

title: Guhilota / Sisodia Dynasty of Mewar
provisions: This dynasty ruled from the 8th century to 1930 CE.
Bappa Rawal and Hamir I were among the founders of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Chittorgarh.

title: Somavamshi Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from the 9th century CE to the 12th century CE.
Mahasivagupta Janmejaya I was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Yayatinagara, present-day Jajpur in Odisha.
Karnadeva was the last ruler of this dynasty.

title: Pala Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 752 to 1150 AD.
Gopala was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Muttagiri, present-day Munger in Bihar.
Gopala was succeeded by Dharmapala.
The Sena dynasty of Lakshman Sena followed this dynasty.
Dharmapala is known for establishing Vikramshila University.
The Arab merchant Suleiman was a contemporary of Dharmapala.
This dynasty was a patron of Buddhism.

title: Pratihara Dynasty (Gurjara-Pratihara)
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 732 to 1030 AD.
Nagabhata I was the founder of this dynasty.
The capitals of this dynasty were Avanti and Kannauj.
Bhoja, also known as Mihira Bhoja, was the greatest ruler of this dynasty.
Mihira Bhoja held the title Adivaraha and was a devotee of Vishnu.
Mihira Bhoja was defeated by Devapala of the Pala dynasty.

title: Rashtrakuta Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 750 to 970 AD.
Dantidurga was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Manyakheta, also known as Malkhed.
Govinda III and Amoghavarsha were the greatest rulers of this dynasty.
This dynasty's feudatories eventually became the Chalukyas of Kalyani.

title: Chola Empire
provisions: This empire ruled from 852 to 1270 AD.
Vijayalaya was the founder of this empire.
This dynasty initially served as feudatories of the Pallavas.
The capital of this empire was Thanjavur.
Aditya I is considered the true founder who established independent power.
Rajendra III was the last ruler of this empire.
Rajaraja I, who ruled from 985 to 1015 AD, is regarded as the greatest ruler of this empire.
Rajendra I ruled from 1015 to 1040 AD and conquered Madurai, northern Sri Lanka, the Ganga kingdom, and Karnataka.
Rajendra I held the title Gangaikonda Chola.
Rajendra I is also known for invading the Srivijaya kingdom.

title: Hoysala Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 1170 to 1340 AD.
Vishnuvardhana was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Dwarasamudra.

title: Kakatiya Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 1110 to 1326 AD.
Prola Raja II was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Warangal.

title: Western Chalukyas of Kalyani
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 1170 to 1200 AD.
Tailapa II was the founder of this dynasty.
The capital of this dynasty was Kalyani.

title: Delhi Sultanate
provisions: The Delhi Sultanate ruled from 1206 to 1526 AD.

It comprised five successive dynasties: the Slave Dynasty (1206–1290), the Khilji Dynasty (1290–1320), the Tughlaq Dynasty (1320–1414), the Sayyid Dynasty (1414–1451), and the Lodi Dynasty (1451–1526).

title: Slave Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 1206 to 1290 AD.
Qutbuddin Aibak, a slave of Muhammad Ghori, was the founder of this dynasty.
Qutbuddin Aibak held the title Lakh Baksh.
Kaikabad was the last ruler of this dynasty.
The succession of rulers was: Qutbuddin Aibak → Shamsuddin Iltutmish → Ruknuddin → Razia Sultana → Bahram Shah → Masood Shah → Nasiruddin Mahmud → Ghiyasuddin Balban → Kaikabad.

title: Khilji Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 1290 to 1320 AD.
Jalaluddin Khilji was the founder of this dynasty.
Khusrau Khan was the last ruler of this dynasty.
The succession of rulers was: Jalaluddin Khilji → Alauddin Khilji → Mubarak Shah → Khusrau Khan.

title: Tughlaq Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 1320 to 1414 AD.
Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq was the founder of this dynasty.
The succession of rulers included Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq, Muhammad bin Tughlaq, and Firuz Shah Tughlaq.

title: Sayyid Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 1414 to 1451 AD.
Khizr Khan was the founder of this dynasty.
Alam Shah was the last ruler of this dynasty.
The succession of rulers was: Khizr Khan → Mubarak Shah → Muhammad Shah → Alam Shah.

title: Lodi Dynasty
provisions: This dynasty ruled from 1451 to 1526 AD.
Bahlol Lodi was the founder of this dynasty.
Ibrahim Lodi was the last ruler of this dynasty.
The succession of rulers was: Bahlol Lodi → Sikandar Lodi → Ibrahim Lodi.

title: Vijayanagara Empire
provisions: This empire ruled from 1336 to 1565 AD.
Harihara and Bukka were the founders of this empire.
This empire comprised four successive dynasties: Sangama (1336–1485), Saluva (1485–1505), Tuluva (1505–1570), and Aravidu (1570–1650).
Devaraya II of the Sangama dynasty ruled from 1420 to 1446 AD and held the title Prabhu Devaraya.
The Persian traveller Abdur Razzaq was a contemporary of Devaraya II.
Saluva Narasimha was the founder of the Saluva dynasty.
Immadi Narasimha was the last ruler of the Saluva dynasty.
Vasco da Gama's arrival was contemporary with the Saluva dynasty.
Vira Narasimha was the founder of the Tuluva dynasty.
Krishnadevaraya ruled from 1509 to 1529 AD and held the titles Yavanarajya Sthapanacharya, Abhinava Bhoja, and Andhra Bhoja.
Albuquerque and Babur were contemporaries of Krishnadevaraya.
Achyuta Raya ruled from 1529 to 1540 AD and was the half-brother of Krishnadevaraya.
The Portuguese traveller Nuniz was a contemporary of Achyuta Raya.
Sadasiva Raya was the last ruler of the Tuluva dynasty, with real power exercised by Rama Raya.
The Aravidu dynasty ruled from 1570 to 1650 AD with Tirumala Raya as its ruler.
The capital of the Aravidu dynasty was Penukonda.

title: Bahmani Kingdom
provisions: Alauddin Hasan Bahman Shah was the founder of this kingdom.
The capitals of this kingdom were Gulbarga and later Bidar.
Tajuddin Firoz Shah was the greatest ruler of this kingdom.
Ahmad Shah Wali was the last ruler of this kingdom.

title: Berar Kingdom
provisions: This kingdom was founded in 1484 AD.
Fatahullah Imad Shah was the founder of this kingdom, establishing the Imad Shahi dynasty.
This kingdom was annexed by Ahmadnagar in 1574 AD.

title: Bijapur Kingdom
provisions: This kingdom was founded in 1489 AD.
Yusuf Adil Shah was the founder of this kingdom, establishing the Adil Shahi dynasty.
This kingdom was annexed by Aurangzeb in 1686 AD.

title: Ahmednagar Kingdom
provisions: This kingdom was founded in 1490 AD.
Malik Ahmad was the founder of this kingdom, establishing the Nizam Shahi dynasty.
This kingdom was annexed by Shah Jahan in 1633 AD.

title: Golconda Kingdom
provisions: This kingdom was founded in 1518 AD.
Quli Qutb Shah was the founder of this kingdom, establishing the Qutb Shahi dynasty.
This kingdom was annexed by Aurangzeb in 1687 AD.

title: Bidar Kingdom
provisions: This kingdom was founded in 1526 AD.
Amir Ali Barid was the founder of this kingdom, establishing the Barid Shahi dynasty.
This kingdom was annexed by Bijapur in 1610 AD.
